summary: The post distinguishes between user-valued work (features, documentation, and support that directly affect users) and developer-valued work (technical debt, CI, internal documentation, and unreleased features). Rocklin argues that while developer-valued work is important, its value is derived entirely from enabling better user-valued work. Teams are ultimately judged on user-facing outcomes, and prolonged focus on either type exclusively signals organizational dysfunction. A healthy team maintains balance between both categories.
key_points: User-valued work directly solves user pain or creates delight, including features, documentation, and direct support
Developer-valued work (technical debt, CI, design docs, unreleased features) is only valuable insofar as it accelerates future user-valued delivery
Teams are judged by users and management on user-valued work in the long term
Exclusive focus on user work signals short-term pressure; exclusive focus on developer work signals weak prioritization
A week or month of developer-focused work is healthy, but beyond that it becomes questionable
money_quotes: Developer valued work is valuable, but only in that it makes us faster in delivering user-valued work to users.
We are judged as a team on user-valued work in the long term.
It's great to do developer-valued work for a week or a month, but beyond that it comes into question.
Focusing entirely on user-focused work tends to be a sign that the team is under short-term pressure.
Focusing entirely on developer-focused work for months at a time may be a sign that the team lacks a strong prioritization function.
key_insight: Developer-valued work has no intrinsic value—it only matters when it enables the team to deliver more user-valued work over time.
